coredata.dc:title: Plasmon-enhanced bulk charge separation via morphological and interfacial engineering in Au@carbon dots@CdS hybrid
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Linjia
Zhang, Rui
Lin, Yanhong
Wang, Dejun
Xie, Tengfeng
coredata.dc:description: The exploitation of morphological engineering strategies enables the design of semiconductor-based photocatalysts with a precision that significantly enhances their photocatalytic activity. In this study, a paradigm has been provided that embeds plasmonic nanostructures within the bulk of the semiconductor, which demonstrates an improvement in interfacial charge transfer and separation. It involves a distinct core-double shelled Au@carbon dots@CdS hybrid, exhibiting excellent photocatalytic H2 generation under the visible light radiation. The interfacial engineering between the plasmonic nucleus and the semiconductor shell has been performed by the incorporation of carbon dots as faster charge channels. The effective driving force for plasmon-enhanced bulk charge separation of semiconductors and the unhindered interfacial charge transmission derived from carbon dots have been demonstrated by the use of steady state surface photovoltage (SPV) and transient state surface photovoltage (TPV) methods, as well as some intrinsic kinetics information of interfacial photoexcited charge carriers transfer processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bulk charge separation
Plasmon-enhanced photocatalysis
Carbon dots
Interface charge transfer
Surface photovoltage

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots-supported Au nanoparticles
Synthesis of individual Au nanoparticles by sodium citrate
Synthesis of core-double shelled Au@CDs@CdS nanoparticles
Synthesis of pure CdS and Au@CdS nanoparticles
Test of steady state surface photovoltage (SPV) and transient state surface photovoltage (TPV) spectra
Morphology and characterization of core-double shelled Au@CDs@CdS NPs
Plasmon-enhanced photocatalytic H2 production properties of Au@CDs@CdS
Mechanism of bulk charge separation in core-double shell nanostructured Au@CDs@CdS hybrid system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Diagrammatic illustration of the two-step synthesis processes of the core-double shelled Au@CDs@CdS NPs. TEM images of (b) initial CDs, (c) core-shelled Au@CDs and (d) core-double shelled Au@CDs@CdS NPs, respectively. HRTEM images of (e) CDs, (f) Au@CDs and (g) the interface region among the Au core and CDs-CdS double shell with fringe spacings.

(a) UV − vis absorption spectra in aqueous solution and (b) XRD patterns of pure CdS, Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS NPs, respectively. XPS spectra of Au@CDs@CdS NPs: (c) full survey spectrum, (d) Au 4f, (e) Cd 3d, (f) S 2p.

(a-b) The results of photocatalytic hydrogen production rates of pure CdS, core-shelled Au@CdS, double shelled Au@CDs@CdS and physically mixed CdS + Au@CDs under visible light irradiation (λ > 395 nm and λ > 550 nm). (c) SPV and (d) corresponding phase spectra of pure CdS, Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS.

(a) TPV and (b) surface photocurrent (SPC) spectra of CdS, Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS NPs. (c) UPS spectra and (d) the plots of (ahν)2 vs hν calculated from the absorption spectrum of CDs, indicating a Eg of 3.22 eV. And HOMO = -(21.21–17.02 + 1.48) eV = -5.67 eV, Eg is 3.22 eV. Therefore, the LUMO is −2.45 eV. (e) Schematic energy level diagram of Au, CDs and CdS in Au@CDs@CdS hybrid system.
Schematic illustration of the separation and migration of electrons and holes in the interface of the (a) Au@CdS and (b) Au@CDs@CdS hybrid system.

head.ce:title: Plasmon-enhanced bulk charge separation via morphological and interfacial engineering in Au@carbon dots@CdS hybrid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Plasmon-enhanced bulk charge separation and migration at Au-CdS interfaces through CDs as the quicker transfer channel, and it is proved that the regulated charge movement pathway and migration dynamic could efficiently accelerate the photocatalytic hydrogen production rate via morphological and interfacial engineering.

The exploitation of morphological engineering strategies enables the design of semiconductor-based photocatalysts with a precision that significantly enhances their photocatalytic activity. In this study, a paradigm has been provided that embeds plasmonic nanostructures within the bulk of the semiconductor, which demonstrates an improvement in interfacial charge transfer and separation. It involves a distinct core-double shelled Au@carbon dots@CdS hybrid, exhibiting excellent photocatalytic H2 generation under the visible light radiation. The interfacial engineering between the plasmonic nucleus and the semiconductor shell has been performed by the incorporation of carbon dots as faster charge channels. The effective driving force for plasmon-enhanced bulk charge separation of semiconductors and the unhindered interfacial charge transmission derived from carbon dots have been demonstrated by the use of steady state surface photovoltage (SPV) and transient state surface photovoltage (TPV) methods, as well as some intrinsic kinetics information of interfacial photoexcited charge carriers transfer processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic water splitting has piqued researchers' curiosity as a potential solution to the energy crisis [1]. In the past decade, semiconductor-based photocatalysis has played a key role in obtaining hydrogen fuel from water based on its ability to convert light energy to chemical energy [2]. However, the efficiency of the process substantially relies on both the capacity of light absorption as well as photo-excited charge carriers' efficient bulk and surface separation, particularly in the visible light region [3]. To resolve these issues, strategies like the rational design of composite construction have been proposed for the improvement of light absorption capacity and regulation of charge separation [3].

Noble metals (primarily Au, Ag, and Cu) have distinctive plasmonic structures and allow excellent photon absorption from the ultraviolet (UV) through visible and near-infrared (NIR) ranges, allowing them to be used as effective photocatalytic materials [4]. According to most of the previous reports, surface modification of semiconductor using plasmonic metal NPs as a co-catalyst is a traditional strategy to increase photocatalytic efficiency under visible light because hot electrons can be directly excited by the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect in plasmonic metals and then transferred into the conduction band (CB) of semiconductors to drive photocatalytic reactions [5]. Besides, the noble metals can act as the trap for the photo-induced electrons on the surface of semiconductors while increasing the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs [6]. However, according to the fundamentals of charge separation involved in semiconductor-based photocatalytic reactions, recombination of electron-hole pairs in the bulk (several ps) is exceedingly rapid in comparison to the charge transfer and consumption processes occurring on the surface, even the recombination on the surface of photocatalyst (ten of ns) [7,8]. Thus, incorporating the appropriate kind of noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) into the bulk of a semiconductor is the most effective strategy technique to achieve charge separation via morphological engineering [9]. Among them, the plasmonic metal core-semiconductor shelled nanostructure demonstrates unique photocatalytic performance, which ensures the intimate interface, significantly amplifying the SPR enhancement effect and the interfacial charge separation from the bulk of the semiconductor [10–12]. However, a comprehensive understanding of the interfacial photo-induced charge delivery kinetics is still lacking, and accurate and effective morphological and interfacial engineering strategies must be investigated in greater depth to accelerate the collection of electrons for the final photocatalytic processes.

Herein, a framework for the rational design of composite photocatalysts for plasmon-enhanced bulk charge separation and transmission strategies are presented. Au@carbon dots@CdS (Au@CDs@CdS) hybrid photocatalyst with unique and distinct interfacial nanostructure of plasmonic core, an interlayer of charge transfer channel and semiconductor outer-shell has been produced using the aforementioned methodologies. Comparing the H2 production capacity of a range of photocatalysts under visible light irradiation (λ > 395 nm and λ > 550 nm) demonstrates the contribution of the plasmon enhancement effect to the light utilization efficiency, particularly in the long wavelength region (λ > 550 nm). Through steady-state surface photovoltage (SPV) and transient state surface photovoltage (TPV) methodologies, some intrinsic kinetics information on the charge migration dynamic of bulk separation has been obtained, shedding light on the fundamentals of charge separation and transfer processes, such as the direction of charge separation and interfacial charge transfer efficiency between Au and CdS. In addition, the interfacial engineering of constructing quicker charge transfer channels is accomplished by the incorporation of carbon dots (CDs) as the middle layer based on the well-matched energy level of Au, CDs and CdS. The regulated charge movement pathway and migration dynamic significantly enhance the interfacial electron transfer rate efficiently. SPV and TPV results of mechanically mixed CdS + Au@CDs NPs (Au@CDs NPs were used as cocatalyst modified on the surface of CdS) also indicate that the intimate interface based on core-shelled nanostructure via morphology engineering is the key to the enhanced charge separation driving force and fluent charge transfer processes, further demonstrating the possibility of regulating the charge separation processes from the bulk of semiconductor as the basis of plasmon effect.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O) and ethylenediamine were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. Citric acid (CA) (C6H8O7·H2O), sodium citrate (EDA) (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O) and sodium sulfite (Na2SO3) were obtained from Beijing Chemical Works, ascorbic acid (AA) and cadmium acetate ((CH3COO)2Cd·2H2O) were bought from Aladdin. Sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O), thiourea and Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) were achieved from XiLong Scientific and Energy Chemical. All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Deionized water is used throughout the experiment.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared with the similar procedure based on the previous report [13]. In detail, CA (1.0507 g) and EDA (335 µL) were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. Then, the solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled to room temperature. The brown-black and transparent product was subjected to dialysis in order to obtained the CA-EDA CDs powders.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots-supported Au nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for synthesizing the carbon dots-supported Au nanoparticles (Au@CDs NPs) is as follows: A mixed 30 mL of aqueous solution consisting of HAuCl4 (0.2 mM), NaOH (0.01 M), CA-EDA CDs (33 mg L-1) was kept at a temperature of 120 °C in an oil bath for 1 h until a stable dark fuchsia suspension was produced. Then the suspension was centrifuged and washed with deionized water for another two times to remove the excess CDs, after that, the sample was redispersed in 10 mL deionized water. The optimized Au@CDs NPs are spherical core–shell structures with a mean diameter of ca. 16 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of individual Au nanoparticles by sodium citrate
ce:section.ce:para: Individual Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) with an average diameter of ca. 16 nm were prepared by the reduction of an aqueous solution of HAuCl4 with sodium citrate, which has been reported by G. Frens [14]: Typically, 2.42 mL of HAuCl4 solution (1 wt%) was added into 200 mL deionized water. After the solution was boiled, 6 mL of sodium citrate solution (1 wt%) was injected into the solution to obtain the Au NPs. Finally, a certain amount of the Au NPs were redispersed in deionized water to prepare suspension with the same concentration of optimized Au@CDs NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of core-double shelled Au@CDs@CdS nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The core-double shelled Au@CDs@CdS nanoparticles (Au@CDs@CdS NPs) were synthesized according to a seeded-growth hydrothermal method: 2 mL of the as-obtained Au@CDs suspension was introduced into 10 mL of distilled water and ultrasonicated for 5 min until a uniform solution was obtained. Then, 560 μL of AA (0.1 M), 560 μL of (CH3COO)2Cd (0.1 M) and 840 μL of thiourea (0.1 M) aqueous solution with 4 mL of CTAB (0.01 M) were added to the above solution. The mixture was then transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 5 h. After cooled to room temperature, the solid particles (Au@CDs@CdS NPs) were collected by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times, and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven. By changing the added volumes of Au@CDs aqueous solution by 1, 2 and 4 mL, different Au@CDs@CdS-x NPs (x: 8, 15 and 20, determined by the CdS shell thickness in nanometer) were obtained, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pure CdS and Au@CdS nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Pure CdS and Au@CdS NPs were synthesized by a similar hydrothermal process to the synthesis of Au@CDs@CdS NPs, using pure H2O or individual Au NPs aqueous solution with the same concentration in replacement of Au@CDs NPs, respectively.

The morphologies of photocatalyst were observed on a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM) operated at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was collected on a Rigaku/Ultima IV diffraction instrument with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å, 50 kV, 200 mA). UV–vis spectra were obtained from Shimadzu UV-3600 UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired from Bruker VERTEXV spectrophotometer (80 V). The fluorescence spectra (steady-state photoluminescence (PL), and time-resolved photoluminescence spectra (TRPL)) were recorded on a PL spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, FLS920). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained from a Thermo ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer with Al Ka X-ray radiation.

The photocatalytic performance of all the photocatalysts was based on the H2 evolution rate, where Na2S and Na2SO3 were adopted as sacrificial reagents and no cocatalyst. Typically, 10 mg of catalysts were dispersed into Na2S (0.35 M) and Na2SO3 (0.25 M) aqueous solution by 30 min ultrasonic treatment, respectively. Before the photocatalytic reaction, the mixed solution was transferred to a quartz reactor with a volume of 50 mL and deaerated with N2 bubbling for 30 min. Finally, the reactor was illuminated under a Xe lamp (300 W) with a 395 nm cut-off filter (λ > 395 nm) and a 550 nm cut-off filter (λ > 550 nm), in which circulating ice-cold water was used to maintain the reaction temperature. The hydrogen production was evaluated by gas chromatography (SHMADAZU GC-2014) with a 5 Å molecular sieve column and a thermal conductive detector with N2 as the carrier gas. The testing for each sample was repeated at least 3 times to guarantee its reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test of steady state surface photovoltage (SPV) and transient state surface photovoltage (TPV) spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The SPV spectrum was employed to analyze the photo-induced charge carrier behavior of samples on our homemade equipment. The experimental setup for the SPV spectrum was composed of a 500 W Xe lamp (CHF-XM-500 W), a monochromator (ZLolix SBP500), a lock-in amplifier (SB830-DSP) with a light chopper (SR540) and a computer. The testing frequency was fixed as 24 Hz, while the scanning wavelength ranged from 300 to 800 nm. Equipment for TPV spectra was assembled by our group, Including a 500 MHz digital phosphor oscilloscope (TDS 5054, Tektronix) and a laser pulse (Polaris II, New Wave Research, Inc). ‘ITO-sample-ITO’ configuration was adopted for testing. SPV and TPV spectra of each sample have been measured at least 3 times to give the right tendency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and characterization of core-double shelled Au@CDs@CdS NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The core-double shelled Au@carbon dots@CdS nanoparticles (Au@CDs@CdS NPs) were fabricated using a two-step synthetic protocol to achieve accurate management of nanostructures (as depicted in Fig. 1 a). Based on a prior report [13], citric acid and ethylenediamine were used to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) using a typical hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 8 h. In a nutshell, CDs containing a lot of reductive groups showed the ability to directly reduce Au3+ to Au NPs by incubating HAuCl4 for 1 h at 120 °C in an oil bath. As a capping agent, CDs could coat the surface of the Au nucleus in the interim to form core-shelled Au@CDs NPs [15]. To synthesis Au@CDs@CdS NPs that resemble the core-double shelled nanostructure, with Au@CDs NPs serving as the nuclei and CdS making up the outer shell, a synthetic approach adapted from the seeded-growth method was developed. The core-double shelled Au@CDs@CdS NPs could be produced using a conventional hydrothermal process at 120 °C for 5 h, as shown in Fig. 1a.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was employed to examine the morphology and structure of initial CDs, the as-prepared core–shell structured Au@CDs and Au@CDs@CdS NPs. Fig. 1b distinctly demonstrates the mono-dispersed dispersion of CDs. With an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, the lattice fringes of CDs in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1e) are visible and are significantly closer to (100) graphite carbon diffraction facets [16]. The synthesized Au@CDs NPs are uniform and well-dispersed, with an average size of 16 nm, as shown in Fig. 1c. The lattice spacing of 0.24 and 0.21 nm, which are attributed to (111) and (100) diffraction facets of face-centered cubic Au and graphite carbon, respectively, are located at the center and edge of the spherical nanostructure (Fig. 1f) [15], further confirming the modification of CDs on the surface of the Au nucleus. Based on Au@CDs, the final core-double shell nanostructured Au@CDs@CdS NPs were fabricated using a straightforward seed-growth synthesis process. Besides, a variety of raw material feedings can be used to adjust the CdS outer shell width. By adjusting the feeding amounts of Au@CDs NPs, we successfully obtained three prototypical NPs of Au@CDs@CdS with remarkable uniformity which are denoted as ACC-8, ACC-15 and ACC-20 NPs (here, the number refers to the thickness (nm) of CdS outer-shell, and Au@CDs@CdS NPs represents ACC-15 NPs in this paper unless otherwise specified). As seen in Fig. 1d, the well-formed, light-colored CdS shell's thickness is around 15 nm, and the dark Au@CDs NPs fit inside it snugly. The lattice fringes with 0.24, 0.33 and 0.21 nm spacing correspond to the (111), (002) and (100) diffraction planes of Au, CdS and graphite carbon have been highlighted by the parallel lines in the HRTEM image of ACC-15 NPs, respectively (Fig. 1g) [17]. And the TEM images of ACC-8 and ACC-20 NPs have been shown in Figure S1.

To gain an in-depth understanding of the unique plasmonic properties of Au@CDs, a detailed set of characterization of CDs and Au@CDs has been represented in Figure S2. UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and Au@CDs composite NPs in an aqueous solution have been shown in Fig. S2 a. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs gives two absorption peaks at 238 and 340 nm, that are assigned to the π-π* and the n-π* transition, respectively. For the Au@CDs, an analogous peak of n-π* transition also appears at 340 nm and a shoulder peak is seated in the range of 225–285 nm associated with π-π* transition of sp2 carbon. In addition, a characteristic peak at 520 nm consistent with the SPR absorption band of Au appears in the spectrum of Au@CDs, indicating the formation of the composite nanostructure. In comparison to individual Au NPs with a size of roughly 16 nm (Fig. S2b), the SPR absorption band of Au@CDs is significantly extended due to the particular design of the CDs shell, thereby enhancing the light absorption capabilities in the visible light range. In the FTIR analysis of CDs (Fig. S2c), the information regarding the surface functional groups of CDs is observed: stretching vibrations of NH were observed around 3100–3300 cm−1 and those for OH at 3400–3700 cm−1, stretching vibration of CH at 1394, 2900 and 2947 cm−1. Fig. S2d expressed the detail information of CDs’ surface groups in the range of 1000–2000 cm−1: stretching vibrations of –COOH at 1750 cm−1, stretching vibrations of CO, CC at 1700 and 1650 cm−1 and bending vibrations of CN at 1560 cm−1, respectively [18,19]. In the FTIR spectrum of Au@CDs, signals of stretching vibration of CH still can be observed but the peaks of stretching vibrations of NH and bending vibrations of CN disappeared, indicating the ability of electron-donating group, –NH2 especially may be the active sites for reduction of gold ions [20]. Additionally, a new peak at 1626 cm−1 appears, corresponding to the vibration signal of carboxylate ions (COO−), thus CDs bind with Au nucleus mainly by COO-Au coordination bond [21,22]. These information about above functional groups of CDs and Au@CDs has been illustrated in Table S1. By using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, surface group information on CDs and Au@CDs was also obtained. The detailed analysis of the C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and Au 4f analysis revealed various types of atoms and the information has been presented in Figure S3 and Table S2. Fig. S3 b and Fig. S3e show that the hybrid's N content has significantly decreased after the generation of Au@CDs, further validating the ability of CDs' electron-donating groups (particularly the –NH2) to convert Au3+ to Au@CDs NPs. And the Au 4f peaks are seen with two doublet components. The high binding energy signals at 84.2 (Au 4f7/2) and 87.8 eV (Au 4f5/2) were linked to the metallic Au, whereas two additional peaks at 83.1 and 86.8 eV were linked to OC-O-Au (Fig. S3c) [23]. Additionally, the O 1 s peak of Au@CDs shifts toward a higher binding energy level (Fig. S3f). The above results once again suggest coordination bonds of COO-Au formed between CDs carboxyl moieties and Au3+ ions in Au@CDs hybrid. Due to their unique surface state, CDs generally exhibit typical excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior [24]. The CDs we obtained inthis study likewise exhibited excitation-dependent PL characteristics, with their emission typically being strongly concentrated in the blue light band (Fig. S4 a). Moreover, as Fig. S4b shows, when CDs are deposited on the surface of Au cores to produce the core-shelled nanostructure, their fluorescence is entirely muted, which is quite exceptional. This could be owing to the charge transfer (CT) activity between CDs and Au, which prevents the excited electrons from the surface state returning to the CDs' ground state andemitting fluorescence [25]. The strong interaction caused by the CT effect among Au cores and CDs shell may be responsible for enhancing the plasmonic properties of Au nucleus in the Au@CDs hybrid system [26].

As demonstrated by Fig. 2a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Au@CDs@CdS NPs in aqueous suspension has been recorded. To more clearly comprehend the physicochemical properties of core-double shelled Au@CDs@CdS NPs, core–shell structured Au@CdS NPs based on unmodified individual Au NPs (Fig. S5 a) have been synthesized. The TEM image of Au@CdS NPs in Fig. S5b shows that the dark-colored Au NPs fit precisely inside the well-formed, light-colored 15 nm CdS shell. However, under the same synthetic reaction conditions, pure CdS NPs had a larger and wider variety of diameter than core–shell structured NPs (Fig. S5c). The growth kinetics of CdS NPs was changed by the incorporation of noble metal cores, expressing the improvement of the hybrids’ homogeneity. Additionally, the CdS outer shell can act as a protective layer to strengthen the stability of plasmonic nucleus in turn. In the UV–vis absorption spectra, upon both of individual Au NPs and Au@CDs NPs being incorporated into the bulk of CdS to form the core-shelled nanostructures, the SPR absorption bands of Au can be observed obviously and exhibited a red shift to 600 nm (compared with that in Fig. S2b) [27], thereby enhancing the light utilization capacity of CdS. More significantly, the expansion of the SPR band in the spectrum of Au@CDs@CdS has also been observed due to the carbon dot modified Au nucleus, which is in agreement with the UV–vis measurement of Au@CDs and individual Au NPs, providing further proof that the incorporation of CDs based on interfacial engineering could amplify the plasmonic activities of Au nucleus in Au@CDs@CdS hybrid system.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) and XPS were also utilized to distinguish the chemical structure of Au@CDs@CdS NPs. Fig. 2b shows the crystalline structures of various photocatalysts including CdS, Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS NPs obtained via XRD measurements. The diffraction peaks of hexagonal CdS (JCPDS no. 06–314) are well identified in the XRD patterns of CdS, Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS samples, and the diffraction peak belonging to the (111) plane of face-centered cubic Au (JCPDS no. 04–784) is observable in the XRD patterns of Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS samples. Whereas, the diffraction peaks of CDs cannot be observed in the XRD patterns of Au@CDs@CdS because of the low content in the samples. The full-range XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c) shows the typical signals of Cd and S for Au@CDs@CdS NPs, whereas, the signal of Au is too weak to obtain due to the cladding of the thick CdS shell. As illustrated in Fig. 2d, high-resolution XPS spectra of the main peaks attributed to the binding energies of Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 can be observed at 83.85 and 87.45 eV [23]. The Cd 3d peaks in Fig. 2e comprise two components: the binding energy signals at 405.59 (Cd 3d5/2) and 412.36 eV (Cd 3d3/2) correlate to the Cd2+ cations. Besides, Fig. 2f depicts the S 2p spectrum. The two peaks at 161.99 (S 2p3/2) and 163.26 eV (S 2p1/2) correspond to the binding energies of the S2- anions [28]. All of these results support the successful preparation of Au@CDs@CdS NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plasmon-enhanced photocatalytic H2 production properties of Au@CDs@CdS
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of various as-prepared photocatalysts was investigated for the generation of H2 under visible light (λ > 395 nm and λ > 550 nm) illumination. In this study, photocatalysts (10 mg) are utilized for all the photocatalytic testing, in which Na2SO3 and Na2S are used as sacrificial agent and no additional cocatalyst. As expressed by Fig. 3a, when exposed to visible light (λ > 395 nm), pure CdS nanospheres exhibit insufficient photocatalysis. Additionally, the Au@CdS NPs with a core-shelled nanostructure exhibit 2.77 times the efficiency of pure CdS in producing H2 at 377 μmol g-1h−1, thereby demonstrating that by effectively utilizing the SPR effect, Au NPs can presumably make it easier to separate photogenerated charge carriers and increase the photocatalytic activity of CdS. Furthermore, upon carbon dots modified Au NPs (Au@CDs) are introduced into the bulk of CdS, the finally prepared Au@CDs@CdS core-double shelled NPs display a soaring photocatalytic activity, attaining the H2 production rates as high as 646 μmol g-1h−1 without any additional cocatalyst (ACC-15), about 2.33 times elevated as to that of Au@CdS NPs. This result indicates that CDs existing at the interface between the plasmonic nucleus and the semiconductor shell can further facilitate the hydrogen-producing efficiency. However, in the Au@CDs@CdS hybrid system, the photocatalytic activity will be affected when the thickness of the CdS outer shell is too thin or thick in ACC-8 and ACC-20 systems [9]. The photocatalytic cycling stability for ACC-15 is exemplified in Figure S6a. The hydrogen production rate of ACC-15 is stable at about 650 μmol g-1h−1 under visible light irradiation (λ > 395 nm) within initial 2 h and the efficiency began to drop as which was illuminated longer than it. The decline in efficiency might be attributable to the loss of sacrificial agent. The stability is also verified via the UV–vis spectra after the catalytic test cycles in Figure S6b. The spectral shape of the photocatalyst after irradiation does not change greatly and the SPR absorption peak of Au can be still observed at 601 nm.

Photocatalytic H2 production under visible light irradiation with longer wavelength (λ > 550 nm) of the aforementioned series of photocatalysts has been carried out in Fig. 3b to confirm the involvement of plasmon-enhanced effect for the photocatalytic activities. First, pure CdS nanospheres generate a minimal H2 to be detected, but core-shelled Au@CdS NPs still exhibits 124 μmol g-1h−1 of H2 evolution rate under longer wavelength light irradiation, demonstrating that plasmonic metal cores are suitable for wider light harvesting in the hybrid photocatalyst. Furthermore, the H2 evolution rate of Au@CDs@CdS is much higher than that of Au@CdS again, reaching 314 μmol g-1h−1. This improvement by introducing CDs might benefit from the cooperation of the enhanced-plasmonic effect based on the extension of the SPR absorption band and interfacial charge transfer efficiency between the Au cores and CDs [26]. As a result, an increasing number of hot electrons induced by the SPR effect migrate from bulk to the surface of the photocatalyst. Additionally, the physically mixed CdS + Au@CDs photocatalyst only slightly improves the photocatalytic efficiency under visible light irradiation (both λ > 395 nm and λ > 550 nm region), which is in sharp contrast to that observed in the case of core–shell structured Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS NPs. This result demonstrates that the construction of plasmonic core–semiconductor shelled nanostructure can effectively provide an intimate interface to maximize the plasmon-enhanced effect for promoting photocatalysis.

After that, steady state surface photovoltage (SPV) technique were utilized to characterize the photo-induced carrier transfer processes in the Au@CDs@CdS hybrid system. The SPV mainly represents the photo-induced carrier transfer dynamics taking place on the CdS surface. It is based on precise composite nanostructures that have a plasmonic Au core, a CDs-based middle layer, and a CdS outer shell. To put it another way, the incorporation of the plasmonic metal nucleus is equivalent to the act of regulating the charge separation behavior from the direction of the CdS bulk phase.

The SPV spectrum of pure CdS has shown in Fig. 3c. This spectrum reveals two sections of reversed polarity SPV signals: the first spans the wavelength range of 300 to 440 nm, whereas the second spans the wavelength range of 440 to 700 nm [29,30]. The first region reveals the band-band transition in photogenerated carriers, demonstrating that photogenerated holes can move to the surface under light irradiation (generated a photovoltage phase value near 0° in Fig. 3d), and the second region is due to the electron trapping of surface state (phase value near −180°), which can be found in the majority of semiconductor materials [31]. The fact that the first SPV area of CdS broadens from 440 to 500 nm when Au cores (both individual Au and Au@CDs NPs) are inserted to afford core-shelled hybrids is the evidence indicating the improvement of light absorption capacity of the hybrids. Meanwhile, the signal of electron trapping of the surface state disappears, which may be caused by the change of surface properties of CdS after the formation of the composite structure in our previous work [32]. A new negative SPV response appears at approximately 500 nm in the spectra of Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS (the inset of Fig. 3c), which corresponds with the phase spectra in Fig. 3d. And Figure S7a displays the SPV spectra of individual Au and Au@CDs NPs, which were collected to provide an in-depth understanding of the influence of plasmonic cores on core-shelled hybrid systems. The negative photovoltage signals are observed from 300 to roughly 500 nm in both the SPV spectra of individual Au and Au@CDs NPs, indicating plasmonic hot electrons will transfer onto the surface, corresponding to the phase value at −90° ∼ −180° in Figure S7b. The phase signal of Au@CDs NPs ends at a longer wavenumber (530 nm), which further indicates the enhancement of the plasmon effect by CDs. However, the photon-generated carrier formed by the SPR effect has a very limited lifespan [33], which results in the SPV signals being extremely weak. These findings provide evidence that the peaks emerging around 500 nm in the SPV spectra of Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS in Fig. 3c are due to the SPR effect of Au nuclues.

Fig. 3c also reveals an interesting phenomenon:the maximum positive intensity of the photovoltage signals of pure CdS, Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS diminish gradually. According to our previous work, upon contacting Au and CdS to form composite Au@CdS, the direction of the interfacial electric field is from CdS towards Au, as their work function (WF(Au) > WF(CdS)). Photoinduced electrons from the semiconductor will migrate to the surface and holes to the bulk under the influence of interfacial electric fields when the Au@CdS NPs are exposed to radiation (Elight > Eg of CdS) [34]. On the other hand, the SPR effect can be used to filter excited electrons via the energy barrier created by a Schottky contact, avoiding recombination with holes and promoting even greater number of plasmonic electrons transfer from plasmonic metal to the semiconductor [35]. As a result, there are fewer photogenerated holes and more photogenerated electrons on the surface of CdS, which results in a clear fall in SPV intensity. Furthermore, the Au@CDs@CdS hybrid system experiences a sharp drop in SPV intensity, supporting the increased effectiveness of bulk charge separation.

On the foundation, CdS shell dependence of plasmon-enhancement has been investagated as well in the Au@CDs@CdS hybrid system. As shown in Figure S8a, the SPR absorption peak causes a red shift as the CdS shell thickness increases from 8 to 15 and 20 nm. The behaviour of carriers’ during transfer is also significantly influenced by the width of the shell. The maximum SPV intensity is observed at ACC-8 nm, as shown in Figure S8b, confirming the effectiveness of weak plasmon-induced hot electron injection. The SPV intensity then falls when the CdS shell width increases from 8 to 15 nm. Additionally, the SPV strength slightly decreases when the CdS shell thickness rises from 15 to 20 nm, following the same general pattern as the observed CdS thickness dependency of the H2 evolution rate in Fig. 3a. According to earlier research, SPR-stimulated hot electrons can only participate in the photocatalytic reaction when they cross over the Schottky barrier at the interface between metal and semiconductor [36]. A thicker shell reduces the Schottky potential, which improves the injection process, but it also results in fewer initial high-energy hot electrons, which lowers the efficiency of hot electron injection [36]. In conclusion, the complex nanostructures' rational and elaborate architecture is the foundation for extremely efficient H2 production.

For further illuminating the function of CDs for improving charge transfer efficiency, TPV spectra under 355 nm pulse laser are also provided in Fig. 4a, further validating the aforesaid photogenerated charge separation and transfer mechanism. The variable regularity of the signal strength is the same with SPV spectra under both short and long testing times. A further indication that CDs can speed up the pace of charge transport between the Au core and CdS shell is the fact that the charge extraction process of Au@CDs@CdS (t3) is quicker than that of pure CdS (t1) and Au@CdS (t2). Since there are the most photoinduced electrons on the surface for photocatalysis, the Au@CDs@CdS photocatalyst in Fig. 4b shows the highest photocurrent intensity, demonstrating that the charge separation in the hybrid system is amplified by CDs' establishment of charge transfer channels at the interface of the Au core and CdS shell.

Separation and transfer efficiency of photoexcited electrons and holes in the different photocatalysts was further evaluated by the PL spectroscopy (Figure S9). As shown in steady PL spectra, pure CdS exhibits a very strong emission band around 540 nm at 390 nm light excitation (Figure S9a). In contrast, the fluorescence emissions of both Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS are almost completely quenched, indicating that the introduction of plasmonic Au cores can effectively inhibit the recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes. In addition, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectroscopy has also been used to elucidate the interfacial charge transfer kinetics at these engineering heterogeneous interfaces (Figure S9b). In order to make the comparison on the PL decay kinetics, the average lifetime (τaverage) was calculated using the following equation based on a tri-exponential kinetics model [37,38] (the detailed calculation process has been expressed in the Table S3):
τ
a v e r a g e
=
A 1
τ 1
2
+
A 2
τ 2
2
+ A
3
τ 3
2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
+
A 3
τ 3

The τaverage of CdS, Au@CdS and Au@CDs@CdS are 1.93, 0.54 and 0.36 ns, respectively. Obviously, shortest decay lifetime of Au@CDs@CdS clearly indicates faster interfacial electron transfer processes based on the establishment of CDs charge transport channels, corresponding to the results of TPV spectra [38].

As well known, charge separation and transfer efficiencies depend on the alignment of energy levels. As shown in Fig. 4c-d, ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) reveals that the CDs highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) lie at −5.67 eV and −2.45 eV, respectively. CdS has a −6.38 eV valence band (VB) minimum and a −3.98 eV conduction band (CB) maximum, respectively. Additionally, Au has a Femi level of −5.10 eV [34]. As a result, staggered energy levels between the Au cores, CDs middle layer and CdS outer-shell are generated (Fig. 4e), acting as a driving force for hole transfer from CdS to Au through CDs and boosting electron transfer based on the SPR effect in the opposite direction.

The charge separation driving force from bulk, arising as a resultof core-double shelled nanostructure can be further explored with the help of the SPV and TPV methods. According to the TEM micrograph of CdS + Au@CDs in Figure S10a, Au@CDs NPs are observed to combine on the surface of CdS nanospheres through physical mixture process. However, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CdS + Au@CDs in an aqueous solution exhibits no typical SPR absorption band, similar to the pure CdS. (Figure S10b). Additionally, the SPV spectrum of CdS + Au@CDs exhibits a slight decrease in signal intensity when compared to that of pure CdS, but Figure S10c shows that the characteristic signal broadening and phase changing of the SPR effect is still not present, indicating that the plasmonic core-semiconductor shelled nanostructure plays a dominant role in supplying the close interface of effective contact to maximize the SPR effect. Therefore, as shown in Figure S10d, CDs affect the rate of charge transport scarcely (t1 = t4) in the TPV spectra because of the faint contact between Au and CdS. All the above results demonstrate that effective morphological engineering is the foundation demonstrating interfacial engineering in the hybrid nanostructure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of bulk charge separation in core-double shell nanostructured Au@CDs@CdS hybrid system
ce:section.ce:para: According to these experimental mechanistic insights, a plausible bulk charge separation process in the core-double shelled Au@CDs@CdS hybrid system was proposed in Fig. 5. Based on the alignment of energy levels in Fig. 4e, the CdS outer shell can be excited to generate electron-hole pairs and the interfacial electric field between Au and CdS drove electrons transfer to the surface and holes to the bulk. At the Au-CdS interface, the energetic electrons produced by the SPR effect of Au cores can cross the Schottky barrier. And the conduction band (CB) of CdS can accept a large number of energetic hot electrons due to the core–shell nanostructure's maximum effective contact area, which enhances the free electron density on the CdS shell's surface. Additionally, the intermediate CDs layer between the Au core and CdS shell acts as the charge transfer channel (Fig. 5b), accelerating the charge migration even more. To increase the number of surface-reached electrons for photocatalytic water splitting, the recombination efficiency of electron/hole pairs had to be reduced, which was accomplished by the bulk regulation based on the band alignments of Au-CDs-CdS.

In summary, a type of plasmonic core-intermediate layer (charge transfer channel)-semiconductor outer shell nanostructured hybrid system has been designed, using a layer-by-layer assembly method. As a typical paradigm, Au@CDs@CdS NPs have been designed and prepared, which exhibit excellent plasmon-enhanced photocatalytic H2 production properties under visible light irradiation. Using SPV methods, it was found that the Au core-CdS shell nanostructure generated a strong internal electric field that causes the charge carriers to split up and migrate in different directions for effective directional separation in the bulk phase. The foundation for achieving interfacially engineered hybrid nanostructures is provided by rational morphological engineering. As a result, CDs charge transfer channels have been established in the interface between Au nucleus and CdS outer-shell. Based on the TPV results, CDs have a strong regulating effect on accelerating charge transfer rate between core and shell. Additionally, the middle layer of the CDs increased Au's light in the SPR band, further boosting the plasmon-enhanced effect of Au in this hybrid system. With the help of morphological and interfacial engineering, this work furnishes an in-depth understanding of the interfacial electron delivery kinetics for the rational design of plasmonic metal–semiconductor composite nanostructures for maximizing the efficiency of particular catalytic reactions in the environmental area.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support from Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21870063, 22172057).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.139970.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
